1: “Why are times not kept by the Almighty?” — Job’s demand for divine scheduling of justice. 2 Peter 3:8–9: “with the Lord one day is as a thousand years, and a thousand years as one day. The Lord is not slow to fulfill his promise as some count slowness, but is patient toward you, not wishing that any should perish, but that all should reach repentance.” The delay of justice that Job finds incomprehensible is the patience of God aimed at repentance; Revelation 22:12: “behold, I am coming soon.” The “times” are set; Christ returns on the Father’s schedule.
2: Moving boundary stones — the paradigmatic social crime of the ancient world. In the NT, this crime’s spiritual analog is teaching that moves the eternal boundaries of God’s moral law: Matthew 5:17 (“do not think that I have come to abolish the Law or the Prophets; I have not come to abolish them but to fulfill them”). Christ is the one who honors and fulfills the permanent boundaries of God’s law, while the one who moves those boundaries is under judgment (Galatians 1:8–9).
3: Stealing the widow’s ox and the orphan’s donkey — predatory exploitation of the vulnerable. The Incarnation’s specific social context: Jesus was born into poverty (Luke 2:7), lived as a craftsman’s son, associated deliberately with the poor and marginalized. The one who was himself socially marginal (no permanent home, Matthew 8:20; executed between criminals) is the countertype to the predator who steals from the widow and orphan.
4: The poor of the earth hiding together from oppression — the underground existence of the dispossessed. Jesus’ beatitude: “blessed are the meek (praeis, the poor and dispossessed), for they shall inherit the earth” (Matthew 5:5). The poor who hide now will receive the inheritance; the eschatological reversal of Job 24 is the full kingdom that Christ inaugurates and will consummate.
5: The dispossessed poor like wild donkeys foraging in the wilderness — marginal existence outside the structures of social security. The wilderness was also where Jesus was tested (Matthew 4:1–11), where the disciples were sent (Mark 6:31–32 for rest), and where John the Baptist proclaimed the way of the Lord (Luke 3:4). The wild-donkey existence of the marginalized is the world Christ deliberately entered in his incarnation and ministry.
6: Gathering fodder in another’s field, gleaning in the vineyard of the wicked — the survival economics of the dispossessed. Ruth gleaned in Boaz’s field (Ruth 2); Jesus’ disciples plucked grain on the Sabbath because they were hungry (Matthew 12:1). The gleaner in another’s field is the economic precarity that the kingdom of God will transform: the kingdom feast where all are invited and all eat until satisfied (Matthew 22:9–10; Luke 14:21–23).
7: The naked sleeping without clothing through the night, with no covering in the cold — material deprivation that fails human dignity. Matthew 25:36,38: “I was naked and you clothed me.” The eschatological judgment turns on how the community treated the naked: in serving the naked, they served Christ himself. The naked who have no covering are precisely the ones in whom Christ says he was present; clothing them is clothing him.
8: Wet with mountain rains, clinging to the rock for want of shelter — exposure without protection. The rock as the only available shelter for the destitute is the theological image of God as rock (Psalms 18:2; Job 31:3; Job 62:2). Christ became the permanent shelter for the spiritually destitute: “he who dwells in the shelter of the Most High will abide in the shadow of the Almighty” (Psalms 91:1); Revelation 7:16: “they shall hunger no more, neither thirst any more; the sun shall not strike them, nor any scorching heat.”
9: Orphans snatched from the breast, infants of the poor taken as pledges — child exploitation for debt. The Christ who said “let the little children come to me and do not hinder them, for to such belongs the kingdom of heaven” (Matthew 19:14) is the one who specifically protects and receives those whom the economic system exploits. The children snatched for debt are the countertype of the children welcomed to the kingdom.
10: Going about naked, hungry, carrying the sheaves of grain they produce but cannot eat — the exploitation economy where workers starve amid their own production. The Lord’s Supper specifically addresses this inversion: 1 Corinthians 11:21 (the rich eat and drink while the poor go hungry at the same table). Christ’s institution of the common meal is the specific counter to the economic inversion Job describes.
11: Treading winepresses while thirsty — producing abundance while experiencing deprivation. John 4:13–14: “everyone who drinks of this water will be thirsty again, but whoever drinks of the water that I will give him will never be thirsty again.” The workers who tread wine while thirsty find their thirst permanently satisfied in Christ; he who said “I thirst” from the cross (John 19:28) bore the thirst so that those who come to him need never thirst again.
12: “From the city the dying groan, and the soul of the wounded cries for help; yet God charges no one with wrongdoing” — the cry of the oppressed without divine response. Luke 18:7–8: “will not God give justice to his elect who cry to him day and night? Will he delay long over them? I tell you, he will give justice to them speedily.” The cry that appears unanswered in Job 24:12 receives Christ’s direct promise: the divine response is coming speedily. The apparent divine indifference is the patience before the eschatological judgment.
13: Those who rebel against the light — the moral taxonomy of light-haters. John 3:19–20: “people loved the darkness rather than the light because their works were evil. For everyone who does wicked things hates the light and does not come to the light, lest his works should be exposed.” The light-rebelling wicked of Job 24:13 are precisely the ones who flee the light of Christ; the darkness they prefer is the darkness from which Christ has come to deliver them (1 Peter 2:9: “called out of darkness into his marvelous light”).
14: The murderer rising at dawn to kill the poor — the exploitation of the transitional moment. Christ’s resurrection at dawn (John 20:1: “early on the first day of the week, while it was still dark”) is the ultimate transformation of the dawn: the time when the murderer rises to kill becomes the time when the Life-giver rises from death. The darkness that was the murderer’s ally is permanently defeated by the light of resurrection dawn.
15: The adulterer waiting for twilight — the moral coding of darkness as concealment. Ephesians 5:11–13: “take no part in the unfruitful works of darkness, but instead expose them. For it is shameful even to speak of the things that they do in secret. But when anything is exposed by the light, it becomes visible.” Christ as the light that exposes darkness (John 3:20–21) is the specific answer to the darkness-hiding that the adulterer practices: nothing is hidden from the one who is the light of the world.
16: The thief sealing himself in darkness, not knowing the light — the complete severance from the light. 1 Thessalonians 5:4–5: “but you are not in darkness, brothers, for that day to surprise you like a thief. For you are all children of light, children of the day. We are not of the night or of the darkness.” Christ transforms his followers from darkness-children into light-children; the thief who seals himself in darkness is the anti-type of the community that has been transferred from darkness’ dominion to the kingdom of light (Colossians 1:13).
17: Thick darkness as morning to the light-haters — the complete inversion of the diurnal moral order. At the crucifixion, the moral inversion reached its maximum: darkness at noon (Matthew 27:45), the Light of the World extinguished in apparent defeat. But the Easter morning reversed this: darkness became dawn, death became life. The permanent inversion that the wicked inhabit was temporarily entered by Christ and definitively overturned.
18: The wicked swift on the water’s surface, their portion cursed in the land — the lightness of the wicked who have no substantial root. John 15:6: “whoever does not abide in me is thrown away like a branch and withers.” The rootless wicked who skim the surface are the anti-type of those who abide in Christ, the true Vine, and draw sustenance from his life.
19: Drought and heat consuming the snow waters, Sheol taking those who have sinned — the irreversible movement of the unrooted toward death. 1 Corinthians 15:55–57: “O death, where is your victory? O death, where is your sting? The sting of death is sin, and the power of sin is the law. But thanks be to God, who gives us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ.” The Sheol-consumption that is the end of the sinner is defeated by Christ’s death and resurrection; the sting is removed for those who are his.
20: The womb forgotten, worms feasting on them, no longer remembered — the complete extinction. Hebrews 11:4: “through his faith, though he died, he still speaks.” The righteous are remembered; the wicked’s memory perishes. Christ’s name is eternal: Philippians 2:9–10 (“the name that is above every name”). The worm-eaten forgotten dead are the anti-type of the one who rose bodily and will never see corruption.
21: Exploiting the barren woman and withholding good from the widow — cruelty to the reproductively marginal. The barren who conceives by divine action is a repeated OT type: Sarah, Rebekah, Rachel, Hannah — all barren until God opens the womb. The church, described as the barren woman who now has more children than the married (Isaiah 54:1, quoted in Galatians 4:27), is the ultimate fulfillment: those who had no covenant-status become the family of God through Christ.
22: God dragging the mighty by his power — the sovereign reversal of human strength. Isaiah 40:29–31: “he gives power to the faint, and to him who has no might he increases strength.” 1 Corinthians 1:27: “God chose what is weak in the world to shame the strong.” The dragging-down of the mighty is the same reversal as the Magnificat (Luke 1:52: “he has brought down the mighty from their thrones and exalted those of humble estate”).
23: God giving the wicked security while his eyes watch their ways — the patience of divine watching before judgment. Acts 17:30: “the times of ignorance God overlooked, but now he commands all people everywhere to repent, because he has fixed a day on which he will judge the world in righteousness by a man whom he has appointed.” The divine watching that Job observes is the watching before the appointed judgment; the appointed judge is Christ (Acts 17:31).
24: The wicked exalted for a little while then gone, cut down like heads of grain — the temporary nature of wickedness’ apparent triumph. Revelation 20:10: “the devil who had deceived them was thrown into the lake of fire and sulfur.” The brief exaltation of the wicked — which seems permanent to those who suffer under it — is temporary in the divine perspective. The “little while” of the wicked’s triumph ends at the parousia of Christ, the one appointed to judge.
25: Job’s challenge: who can prove him a liar? The ultimate answer is no one, because Job correctly diagnoses both his own innocence and the empirical reality of unequal divine dealing with the righteous and wicked. In the NT, this is not resolved by argument but by eschatological promise: Revelation 22:12 (“I am coming soon, bringing my recompense with me”). The recompense that Christ brings finally vindicates every Job who suffered innocently and every Eliphaz who misapplied the retributive formula.
